speaker: interviewer
text: --four Esther Barishman. This is August 8, 1995. We're in Laguna Hills, California, USA. OK, so--

speaker: interviewee
text: Where did I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us how you got the money to buy that condominium.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I, I had, we had a camera. So we sold it on the black market. Also, a couple of coins, gold coins, we sold it. Still wasn't enough. But my husband was on the job. And he was-- usually, he was singing and whistling on the job, you know. And then, at once, he was so quiet.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the boss came over and asked him, Boris, what's the matter with you? Something is bothering you? You're not the same person that I know. So he told him, I have opportunity to buy a condominium. And I'm short of so much and so much money. He said, don't worry. I'll lend you the money. And every month, or every payday, I'll take off a few dollars, so you wouldn't even feel it. And you'll have the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's what he did. And this is the way we got the money. And we put a down payment on our condominium in, in Israel, which is-- was in the [NON-ENGLISH] close to [NON-ENGLISH]. It was a very good place to be with, and a single home. By today, that, just the lot alone is a quarter of a million dollars worth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Petah-- near Petah Tikva, [NON-ENGLISH] it calls. It's a [NON-ENGLISH] in, in Petah Tikva. So not only today, 10 years ago, it was a quarter of a million dollars, only the lot. But when we sold it, we got just three tickets to come to Canada for that money. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1953, we sold the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did you decide to come to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it was beginning of, the state of Israel it became. It was hard to make a living. My husband started-- wanted to be a businessman. And he was a installation, a contractor of water installation. And it was short of equipment, it was short of-- everything, was short. And he got fed up with-- you know, he had a hard time to get a proper job and to get some materials for the, for the job. So he was, he got fed up. And he wanted to come to the United States. But I had a cousin in Canada in Winnipeg, not me, my mother's cousin, a second cousin. And he helped out a lot of immigrants to come to Canada, whoever wanted. So when I had wrote him a letter that I would like to come to Canada, he sent me right away, he sent me papers. I just had to sell the house and liquidate the house and come over. So that's what made us come to Canada. Also, my youngest daughter was, was, was hard. She, she got all the sickness that was in Israel, she first got it. So she was developing-- hard to develop.

speaker: interviewee
text: So all combined, we decided to go. And I had a brother in New York, which is close, the only brother. So we wanted to be together more. That is, we came to Canada. And, and Canada and, and New York, it was close already, to be closer together. First you lived in Winnipeg?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in Winnipeg for nine years.

speaker: interviewer
text: From 1953?

speaker: interviewee
text: From 1953 till '62.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, then we moved to Montreal, Canada. And in 1969, we came to California.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the type of--

speaker: interviewee
text: To Los Ange--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in the food business where?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in Winnipeg. We were, we had a grocer-- three different grocery stores in Winnipeg. Then we came to Montreal. I had a grocery store with a, with a, partner, woman partner. And my husband had a factory, which it didn't last too long. It last a year. And we lost all the money that we had in that factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this made us come to United States, because we were penniless and, and shame, you know? And, and he was already at that time 50 years old. So it was hard for him to get a job. And not only hard to get a job, but after you are self-employed for such a long time, it's hard for you to obey orders from somebody else and to go to work. So it was a ad in the paper from Monrovia, from California, that they need some-- no, not Monrovia, Azusa-- that they need some people. They came to Montreal, to put an ad in the papers, they need some people to work, like specialists. My, my husband was a mechanic by trade. So, so he applied for this job. And they said to him, come to Los Angeles. Come to Mon-- Azusa. We hire you right away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's what he did. He pick himself up, and he came to Azusa, got the job. And three months later. I came with my son that time. Because my oldest daughter was studying in Italy. She was-- she is an artist, so she studied art in Italy. My youngest daughter, Shelley, she was in Israel, an exchange student in the University of Jerusalem. And my son was only 14 years old, so he came with us everywhere. So we came to Los Angeles in 1969, the end of '69.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what type of work did you both do here in Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: My, my husband went to his trade as a mechanic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mechanic

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was working in my trade, [LAUGHS] in, in the food business. So I wasn't self-employed anymore. I was just working out. I worked in the [INAUDIBLE] market as a cash-- as a delicatessen. I worked in bakeries. I worked in, in this cashier's, a cashier. I also worked as a new, a nurse for newborn babies for two years in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have nurses training?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I have, I had experience with my own children. [LAUGHS] And I was working as a nurse and also get paid as a nurse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was recommended from a doctor's office, too. So I was, with a white uniform and, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean they would refer people, refer you to people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had a, a nurse that worked in doctor's office. And, and I was taking care of her niece. And she recommend me from, from one to the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that hard working for other people for you, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: After owning your own store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: You had owned your own food stores.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I had, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then was it hard working for other people or was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had some-- from beginning, I have like mama-papa stores. My husband went to work, and I ran the store. When he came back from work, he helped me out. It was like from eight in the morning till midnight, seven days a week and 360 days a year. And this was the first. And then it was different. We had a chain store, too, that we closed at 6 o'clock. And, and the store wasn't already with it. Like the first few stores I had the store in the front of, and the back I had a living quarter.

speaker: interviewee
text: So here I was in the business, here I was at home. When I put on a soup to boil, it became a stew. And-- [LAUGHS] like the-- it all went back and forth. The children went to school. I was-- sent off the children to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then, later, I had already stores that I closed at 6 o'clock and weekends off, you know, so. And delicatessen, it, it's 24 hours. Not 24 hours. It's from six-- from eight to midnight, seven days a week. It's mostly like that, open, even in Canada, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. You said there was a quota even to get into the United States from Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was a quota. But my husband already was in Canada. And we came later. The Canadian are allowed to come to America anytime and stay in America as long as they want, unless they-- but they're not allowed to work. They ought to get a permit of work. So we came as visitors. [BACKGROUND NOISE] But we remain as resident.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were able to?

speaker: interviewee
text: We made, yeah, we made our papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. OK, well that's, that's very good. Now, is there anything that, that I haven't asked you about that you think about, that you'd like to say about what happened during the war or what happened before? Or how you feel about--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we covered it. I think we covered--

speaker: interviewer
text: I think--

speaker: interviewee
text: I just want to, please, show the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: --The pictures. I think one thing I forgot to ask you, though, was you found out later that-- how the people had died in your city, in the ghetto, when the ghetto was liquidated. I don't think we talked about that on tape. Well, we didn't. But I find out that they made a mass grave and put a board over the grave. And the soldier stayed with, where they were shooting right-- let the people walk on board. And they were shooting them, they fell down right, right into the grave. That's the way they, they were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you think that was the Ukrainians that were actually doing the dirty work?

speaker: interviewee
text: The dirty work, the Ukrainian did. They knew, they did the most. Wouldn't be of the Ukrainian, would be less people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And later, there was a, a marker for all the people in the, who had died in that ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: A marker?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was, was there some sort of monument later?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There is a monument in that, that grave. They put a monument on the grave and said that this is the Jews from, from Zdolbunow that are buried.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you, have you been back there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't back there, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I have a picture from the monument, where they-- from the grave that, that it's been made--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you never went back to that town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I never went back.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened-- do you know what happened to that town after the war? What it is now? What country?

speaker: interviewee
text: There is no Jews there. There's one young fellow, yeah, one, one person that remained because he married a Christian woman. And he remains there. But there is no more, nobody there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what country is it part of?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Zdolbunow, in Poland, in, in the same city. There is one--

speaker: interviewer
text: But right after the war, it was occupied by Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's why you didn't want to go back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: It sounds like your father was quite wise with the things that he told you and the foresight that he had.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a very intelligent person, well-educated and intelligent. And he knew, he knew a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this-- what, what went through your head? What were you feeling when your parents would tell you, this might happen. If it does, if we're separated, or you do this, you do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were so ready for the worst during the occupation from the Germans. When they started killing. We heard this, this is already Judenrien. That city has been liquidated. That city is liquidated. We were longer, we kept longer because in our city was a factory from cement. And they put the people working in the cement a lot. And they needed that. So we, we were, we were prolonged. On top of it, we had a German man, a German soldier, that was in charge of the Jewish people, you know. And he was a very good person. He, he was giving a lot, he was-- rescue a lot of Jewish people, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he, he holded on, just like he was one of them, too. He was, he was holding on to the Jews as long as possible, as long as he could. So we were almost from the end, the last cities that were liquidated. I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: In that area.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember his name. I even have a book from him, the one, that, that German soldier that rescue a lot of Jewish people in our city.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Now is there anything that you would like-- any statement you would like to make to people today or future generations, based on what you learned from your life and your experience during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we should all be equal and no, no discrimination of any race, that everybody's a person and everybody's entitled to live and, and have-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to live the life like all the human beings together. That you are Jew or you are Christian or you are Polish or you are a negro and black people, everybody should be equal and enjoy the life of freedom and the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: You still have faith at this point in, in a God or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't believe that there is a God. If it would be a God, he wouldn't let, let this happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you believe before?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not a believer. I'm not religious, and I'm not a believer. I am-- to me, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like I said, if it would be a God, he wouldn't let things happen like the way it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. OK, that's fine. Now we'll just stop for a minute. We'll get the pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go ahead. Tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother and her two sisters. My mother's name was Shifra, and the other sister was Faye and Dincie, the youngest one. I think that was made about 1920 or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of those people survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nobody survived the war. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my parents, my father and mother. I think it was shortly after their marriage. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think so. That's the way I remember them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year would that have been about?

speaker: interviewee
text: It should be, should be in the early '20s, I guess.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in the early '20s.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the way you remember them, young like this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the way I remember them, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my mother and the four children. My oldest daughter-- my oldest sister, Shulamid, my brother, [NON-ENGLISH] Sidney, and myself, and my younger sister, Rachel. And I think that picture is made in 1933, '34, something like that, in the early '30s.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my-- myself and my two sisters, my oldest sister and younger sister, and myself. It's near our house. I think that picture's been made in 1934 or '35, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This and my sister, Shulamid.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was six years older than me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that she was at a Hebrew College in Rovno.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did the whole college close? Did she just have to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the college closed right when, when Hitler came over it was no more school, no more Jews. No Jew had-- was allowed to go to school anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: She came home and went into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she was in ghetto together with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father and my oldest sister, Shulamid. This was-- she came, she was with a school back, from school coming, going in Rovno, in, in high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a college or a high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: A college, I think, college was--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the memorial stone that went on the grave, where the mass grave from the Jews from Zdolbunow are buried. It was made of stone after the war finished. The people, the Jews that came from Russia, the survivors came back to the city and put in a stone.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my brother, Sidney, and his wife, Marilyn, and myself, just recently, about a year ago was it, the picture made in-- when was it? In 1994, in May.

speaker: interviewer
text: Name everyone in the picture and the person who's not there.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my family, except my daughter Sylvia wasn't present at that time. That's my son, Michael with his wife Ettie. My daughter, Shelley with her husband, Charles. And the two grandchildren, Tatiana and Tamara. And at the-- one is only a cousin of ours. And myself in the middle in the that blue dress. It was made also in May of 1994.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
